title: Roman Hispania
subtitle: 27 BC–409 AD · Emperors, Philosophers and Christianisation

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From Augustus's provinces to the emperors born in Hispania

entries.label: Augustus reorganises Hispania — three provinces
entries.hint: Augustus reorganises Hispania into three provinces: Tarraconensis (senatorial-turned-imperial), Baetica (senatorial) and Lusitania (imperial); Emerita Augusta (Mérida) is founded as the capital of Lusitania in 25 BC for veteran soldiers; the reorganisation creates the administrative framework for four centuries.
entries.note: Cassius Dio LIII.12 and 26: primary sources for Augustus's reorganisation. The founding of Emerita Augusta 25 BC: Cassius Dio LIII.26 (primary). The Res Gestae Divi Augusti (Augustus's own account): documents Hispania's place in the empire. The three-province structure: documented in administrative inscriptions throughout Hispania.

entries.label: Trajan and Hadrian — emperors born at Italica
entries.loc: Italica (Santiponce, near Seville) — birthplace of both
entries.hint: Trajan (emperor 98–117 AD) and Hadrian (117–138 AD) are both born at Italica near Seville — the only case in Roman history of two successive emperors from the same city; under Hadrian, Italica is rebuilt as a major imperial city; his lead water pipes stamped with his name survive in situ.
entries.note: Trajan's Italica origins: Historia Augusta, Vita Traiani 1.1 (primary). Hadrian's origins: Historia Augusta, Vita Hadriani 1.1. The Hadrianic city at Italica: archaeology — lead pipes stamped 'IMP HADRIANI' in situ (confirmed by UNESCO tentative list documentation). The Traianeum (temple of Trajan): excavated at Italica. The amphitheatre: one of the largest in the empire — dimensions documented.

entries.label: Theodosius — the last great emperor, born in Hispania
entries.hint: Theodosius I (the Great) is born at Cauca in Hispania and becomes the last emperor to rule both halves of the Roman Empire; he makes Christianity the official state religion (Edict of Thessalonica 380 AD); his death in 395 splits the empire permanently.
entries.note: Theodosius's Cauca origin: Zosimus IV.24 and Pacatus, Panegyric of Theodosius. The Edict of Thessalonica 380 AD (Cunctos populos): Codex Theodosianus XVI.1.2 — the edict making Nicene Christianity the state religion. His death 17 Jan 395: documented. His burial at Milan: documented. Hispania's three emperors (Trajan, Hadrian, Theodosius): the peninsula's most direct contribution to the Roman empire.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Sertorian War, Roman civil wars in Hispania and the Germanic crossing

entries.label: Sertorian War — the rebel province
entries.loc: Hispania (Sertorius's capital at Osca, mod. Huesca)
entries.hint: Quintus Sertorius, a Roman general backing Marius, creates an independent quasi-republic in Hispania using local support — he establishes a Senate, Roman schools for Iberian chiefs' sons, and holds off Pompey and Metellus for eight years; assassinated in 72 BC at a banquet.
entries.note: Plutarch, Life of Sertorius (primary biography). Appian, Civil Wars I.86–115. Sertorius's school at Osca: Plutarch Sert.14 — 'he gathered around him the sons of the most distinguished Iberians...and gave them Latin and Roman culture'. His senate of 300 Romans: Plutarch Sert.22. Konrad (1994), Plutarch's Sertorius: the standard modern study. The Sertorian War is the best-documented example of Roman-Iberian political collaboration.

entries.label: Battle of Munda — Caesar's last battle
entries.hint: Julius Caesar defeats the sons of Pompey at Munda — his most desperately contested battle; Caesar later says he had often fought for victory but at Munda he fought for his life; it is the final military act of the Roman civil wars on Iberian soil.
entries.note: Caesar's own reaction: Suetonius, Divus Julius 36 — 'Caesar said that he had often fought for victory, but at Munda he had fought for his life.' Appian, Civil Wars II.103–105: detailed account. The Bellum Hispaniense (written by an unknown officer, primary): the most detailed account of the Spanish campaign. The battle site: disputed — Montilla or Osuna most commonly identified.

entries.label: The Rhine crossing — Vandals and Suebi reach Hispania
entries.hint: On 31 December 406 the Rhine freezes and Vandals, Alans and Suebi pour across — by 409 they reach Hispania; the Suebi settle in Galicia, the Vandals in Baetica (giving their name to 'Andalusia' via 'Vandalusia'); the Roman province of Hispania effectively ends.
entries.note: The Rhine crossing 31 Dec 406: Jerome, Letter 123.16 — eyewitness account ('all Gaul burned with one fire'). Prosper of Aquitaine, Chronicle: records the crossing's date. The Vandals in Baetica: Hydatius, Chronicle (contemporary Galician bishop) — the primary source for events in Hispania 406–469. The Suebi in Galicia: Hydatius covers this in detail. The name 'Andalusia' from 'Vandalusia': documented in Arabic sources.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Roman municipal law, the Flavian privilege and Christianisation edicts

entries.label: Flavian municipal law — universal Latin rights in Hispania
entries.hint: Vespasian grants the entire Iberian Peninsula the ius Latii (Latin rights) — the single largest extension of Roman citizenship rights in history to that point; it transforms Hispania's population from provincials to Latin citizens with a path to full Roman citizenship.
entries.note: Pliny, Natural History III.30: primary source — 'Vespasian Augustus, in the storms of the state, gave Latin rights to the whole of Spain'. The Lex Irnitana (from the previous folio, c.91 AD): documents the practical implementation of Flavian municipal law. Millar (1977), The Emperor in the Roman World: contextualises the grant's significance. The Lex Malacitana (another Flavian municipal charter): BNE — parallel document.

entries.label: Council of Elvira — the first Western church council
entries.hint: The Council of Elvira (c.306 AD) is the earliest known church council in the Western Mediterranean — 19 bishops and 26 priests attend; its 81 canons cover sexual conduct, idolatry, and Christian-pagan interaction; it is the primary documentary source for Christianity in pre-Constantinian Hispania.
entries.note: The 81 canons of Elvira: preserved in canonical collections; Laeuchli (1972), Power and Sexuality: The Emergence of Canon Law at the Synod of Elvira: the standard English study. The council's date: debated (306 vs 313) — most scholars favour c.306. The bishop list: 19 bishops from across Hispania — documents the geographical extent of early Iberian Christianity. Canon 36 (on images): the earliest documented prohibition of images in a church.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: The martyrs of Hispania and the Christianisation of a province

entries.label: Eulalia of Mérida — the most celebrated Hispanian martyr
entries.hint: Eulalia of Mérida, a 12-year-old girl, is executed during Diocletian's persecution in 304 AD — she becomes the most venerated martyr in Hispania; the poet Prudentius writes her passion (c.400); her basilica at Mérida is documented archaeologically; her cult survives through the Visigothic period.
entries.note: Prudentius, Peristephanon III (c.400 AD): the primary literary account — written within living memory of the martyrdom. The Eulalia basilica at Mérida: documented archaeologically (Mateos Cruz 1999). The Visigothic continuation of her cult: documented in the Vitas Sanctorum Patrum Emeritensium (7th c.). The 304 date: fits Diocletian's persecution decree of that year — documented.

entries.label: Priscillian — heresy, execution and the first Christian to be executed by the state
entries.hint: Priscillian, bishop of Ávila, is executed in Trier in 385 AD — the first Christian to be executed by a Christian Roman emperor for heresy; his followers bring his body back to Hispania; his execution is condemned by Ambrose of Milan and Martin of Tours; it sets a precedent for state religious persecution.
entries.note: Sulpicius Severus, Chronica II.46–51: primary source — contemporary and hostile. Ambrose of Milan's correspondence: condemns the execution. Martin of Tours's opposition: documented in Sulpicius Severus, Life of Martin. The Priscillianist texts (surviving): identified in Würzburg manuscript. Chadwick (1976), Priscillian of Avila: the standard study. The precedent set: documented in subsequent debates about heresy and state power.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: The Roman monuments of Hispania — Mérida, Segovia, Alcántara

entries.label: Theatre of Emerita Augusta — the most complete Roman theatre in Hispania
entries.loc: Emerita Augusta (mod. Mérida, Badajoz) — extant, UNESCO
entries.hint: The Roman theatre of Mérida (16–15 BC) is the best-preserved Roman theatre in Iberia — built by Agrippa, seating 6,000, with a two-storey stage backdrop still standing; still used for the annual Festival de Teatro Clásico; the dedication inscription survives in situ.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage (1993). The dedication inscription: in situ — 'M. Agrippa L.F. cos. III trib. pot. III'. The inscription dates the theatre to 16–15 BC with certainty. Álvarez Martínez (1982), El teatro romano de Mérida: the standard archaeological study. Mérida (Emerita Augusta): the best-preserved Roman city in Iberia — also contains amphitheatre, circus, bridge, arch and aqueduct.

entries.label: Segovia aqueduct — Roman engineering without mortar
entries.hint: The Segovia aqueduct stands 28 metres tall, has 167 arches over 818 metres, and is built entirely without mortar — the precisely cut granite blocks hold each other by friction alone; in continuous use for nearly 2,000 years, it still carried water to Segovia into the 20th century.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage (1985). The 'no mortar' construction: confirmed by modern engineering surveys. Dating: numismatic evidence suggests 1st or early 2nd c. AD — no direct inscription survives. The aqueduct's continuous use to c.1970s: documented in Spanish municipal water records. Bonnin & Raya (2005), El acueducto de Segovia: the standard technical study. The 20,400 individual granite blocks: documented.

entries.label: Alcántara bridge — the most perfect Roman bridge
entries.hint: The Trajan-era bridge at Alcántara spans the Tagus at 71 metres height — its dedicatory inscription survives: 'Pontis perfectionem perpetuam' (to the eternal perfection of this bridge); its architect Lacer dedicates a small temple to the bridge's own immortality; it is the most celebrated Roman bridge in Iberia.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Dedicatory inscription: in situ on the bridge's triumphal arch — records Trajan's reign and the architect Lacer's name. The inscription text: 'Pontem perpetui mansurum in saecula mundi' (a bridge to last through the ages of the world). Blázquez (1965) on Roman bridges in Hispania. The bridge: extant (partially reconstructed after civil war damage). The small temple to the bridge: also extant at one end.

entries.label: Roman circus of Mérida — chariot racing and spectacle
entries.hint: Emerita Augusta's circus (hippodrome) is the longest in Roman Hispania — 430 metres, seating up to 30,000; the circus, theatre and amphitheatre together make Mérida the most complete Roman entertainment complex surviving in Iberia; the circus's spina (central barrier) is documented archaeologically.
entries.note: The Mérida circus: documented in the Conjunto Arqueológico de Mérida. Sánchez-Palencia et al. (2001), El circo romano de Emerita Augusta: the standard study. The seating capacity (30,000): estimated from dimensions. Chariot racing as the primary public spectacle: documented in Roman literary sources throughout the imperial period. The circus's alignment with the street grid: documented in the archaeological plan of Mérida.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Hispania's remarkable literary generation — Seneca, Lucan, Quintilian, Martial

entries.label: Seneca — the Stoic philosopher from Córdoba
entries.hint: Lucius Annaeus Seneca is born in Córdoba and becomes the most influential philosopher of the Roman imperial period — his Letters, Tragedies and Moral Essays are the primary documents of Stoic thought in Latin; he is Nero's tutor and is forced to commit suicide on Nero's orders in 65 AD.
entries.note: Seneca's birth in Corduba: Seneca, Consolatio ad Helviam 17.3 (autobiographical reference). His death: Tacitus, Annals XV.60–64 (primary, detailed, near-contemporary). The Letters to Lucilius: 124 letters — BnF and Bodleian hold medieval manuscripts. The tragedies: first printed edition Ferrara 1484. Wilson (2014), The Greatest Empire: A Life of Seneca: the standard modern biography.

entries.label: Lucan — the epic poet who died for Calpurnius Piso's conspiracy
entries.hint: Marcus Annaeus Lucanus (Lucan) is Seneca's nephew, born in Córdoba — his Bellum Civile (Pharsalia) is the greatest Latin epic after the Aeneid, covering Caesar's civil war without gods; he joins the Pisonian conspiracy against Nero and is forced to commit suicide at 25.
entries.note: Lucan's Córdoba birth: Suetonius, De Poetis (Vita Lucani). The Bellum Civile (Pharsalia): 10 books — earliest manuscripts 9th c., BnF and Monte Cassino. His death 65 AD: Tacitus, Annals XV.56 and 70 (primary). Fantham (1992), Lucan: De Bello Civili Book II (Cambridge): the standard modern commentary. The Bellum Civile's Republican ideology: the reason Nero banned it and the reason it survives — it circulated underground.

entries.label: Martial — the epigrammatist from Bilbilis
entries.loc: Bilbilis (mod. Calatayud, Zaragoza) — born; Rome (career); Bilbilis (retired)
entries.hint: Marcus Valerius Martialis is born in Bilbilis and writes the 12 books of Epigrams that are the primary documentary source for daily life in 1st-century Rome — Latin literature's most direct social observation; he retires to Bilbilis in old age and his later poems document small-town Hispanian life.
entries.note: Martial's Bilbilis origin: Martial, Epigrams X.103 and XII.18 (self-documented). The Epigrams (c.86–102 AD): 12 books; first printed edition Venice 1501. The retirement to Bilbilis: Epigrams XII.18. Sullivan (1991), Martial: the Unexpected Classic: the standard English study. Martial's social documentation: he names over 1,000 individuals — the richest social record in Latin poetry.

entries.label: Quintilian — the rhetoric teacher from Calagurris
entries.loc: Calagurris (mod. Calahorra, La Rioja) — born; Rome (career)
entries.hint: Marcus Fabius Quintilianus is born in Calagurris and becomes Rome's first state-funded professor of rhetoric — his Institutio Oratoria (12 books) is the most comprehensive ancient treatise on education and rhetoric; its recovery by Poggio Bracciolini in 1416 is a landmark of the Renaissance.
entries.note: Institutio Oratoria (c.95 AD): 12 books. The full text recovered by Poggio Bracciolini at the monastery of Saint Gall 1416: documented in Poggio's letters. BnF and British Library hold early manuscripts. Quintilian's state salary: Suetonius, De Grammaticis et Rhetoribus 17 — 'Vespasian was the first to give rhetoric teachers an annual salary of 100,000 sesterces from the treasury'. Kennedy (1969), Quintilian: the standard English study.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Baetican olive oil, Hispanian silver and the peninsula's wealth

entries.label: Dressel 20 amphoras — Baetican oil across the empire
entries.hint: Dressel 20 amphoras produced in the Guadalquivir valley are found across the entire Roman empire — from Hadrian's Wall to Mesopotamia; Monte Testaccio in Rome is a 50-metre hill made entirely of smashed Baetican olive oil amphoras, estimated at 53 million individual containers.
entries.note: Dressel 20 amphoras: the single most distributed amphora type in the Roman world. Monte Testaccio: Rodríguez Almeida (1984) — estimated 53 million amphoras. The stamped handles (tituli picti): carry producer names, dates, weights and inspector marks — the most detailed ancient trade documentation. Mattingly (1988), Oil for Export?: the standard study. Blázquez (1978), Economía de la Hispania romana: the broader economic context.

entries.label: Roman Hispania at its peak — three emperors, five writers
entries.loc: Italica; Corduba; Calagurris; Bilbilis; Emerita Augusta
entries.hint: The period 70–200 AD is Hispania's cultural and political peak — three emperors (Trajan, Hadrian, later Theodosius) and four of Latin literature's greatest writers (Seneca, Lucan, Martial, Quintilian) all come from the peninsula; no other province contributes more to the Roman imperial project.
entries.note: The concentration of Hispanian talent: noted by ancient writers including Juvenal (who mocks Iberian poets flooding Rome). The five writers: all documented as Iberian-born in ancient sources. The three emperors: all documented as Iberian-born in ancient sources. The irony: Hispania, conquered so brutally by Rome, becomes one of Rome's most productive provinces — a primary case study in romanisation.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Trade networks, Stoic philosophy across the empire and the Atlantic route

entries.label: Garum — the fermented fish sauce that conquered Rome
entries.loc: Baelo Claudia (mod. Bolonia, Cádiz); Carthago Nova; Baetica coast
entries.hint: Hispania is the primary producer of garum (fermented fish sauce) for the Roman Empire — factories at Baelo Claudia, Carthago Nova and along the Atlantic coast; the garum of Hispania is considered the finest; its factories are documented archaeologically as the largest food-processing sites in Roman Hispania.
entries.note: Baelo Claudia (extant, Tarifa area): UNESCO — the best-preserved garum factory site in the Roman world, fully excavated. The Scomber fish-sauce amphoras (Dressel 7–11): found across the empire. Curtis (1991), Garum and Salsamenta: the standard study. Ponsich & Tarradell (1965): the foundational archaeological survey of garum factories. Pliny, Natural History XXXI.94: documents Hispanian garum's excellence.

entries.label: Stoic philosophy — the Senecan school and its transmission
entries.loc: Corduba (origin); Rome; later — all of Europe
entries.hint: Seneca's Stoicism, rooted in his Corduban upbringing, becomes the dominant philosophical influence on Western Europe through the Middle Ages and Renaissance — his Letters to Lucilius are the primary vector; Erasmus, Montaigne and Francis Bacon all cite Seneca; his Hispanian origin is a thread connecting ancient and early modern Europe.
entries.note: Seneca's influence on Erasmus: documented in Erasmus's edition of Seneca (1515, 1529). Montaigne's citations of Seneca: throughout the Essays — over 100 references. The manuscript transmission: Reynolds (1965), The Medieval Tradition of Seneca's Letters: the standard study of manuscript history. The Corduba connection: Seneca explicitly describes his Iberian upbringing — Consolatio ad Helviam 17.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Roman Hispania in the context of the empire's rise and fall

entries.label: Hispania's contribution to the Roman empire — and its end
entries.hint: Roman Hispania's 436 years of provincial existence is a story of remarkable integration — from resistance to the peninsula that supplied Rome with its greatest emperors and writers; the irony that the Germanic peoples who end Roman Hispania (Vandals, Suebi) enter the peninsula from the same direction as the Carthaginians and Romans before them.
entries.note: The 436-year span (27 BC–409 AD): documented in continuous administrative records. Hydatius, Chronicle (contemporary, from Galicia): the primary source for the 409 AD crossing and its immediate consequences. The Vandal name and 'Andalusia': documented in Arabic sources as 'al-Andalus' from 'Vandalusia' — though some scholars dispute this etymology. Collins (2004), Visigothic Spain: contextualises the transition.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Columella — Roman agricultural slavery documented from Hispania
entries.loc: Gades (mod. Cádiz) — born; Hispania Baetica (family estate)
entries.hint: Lucius Junius Moderatus Columella is born in Cádiz and writes the most comprehensive Roman agricultural manual (De Re Rustica, 12 books) — it documents in detail the management of enslaved agricultural workers on Baetican estates, including their housing, diet, punishments and reproduction.
entries.note: Columella, De Re Rustica (c.60 AD): 12 books. BnF and other libraries hold medieval manuscripts. His Gades origin: Columella I Praef.1. The chapters on enslaved workers: Book I.6–9 — 'the management of slaves' (primary documentation of agricultural slavery conditions). Ash (1941), Columella translation (Loeb): the standard English edition. The Baetican context: Columella draws on personal experience of Baetica — primary evidence for the province's agricultural organisation.

entries.label: Roman women in Hispanian inscriptions — the epigraphic evidence
entries.loc: Emerita Augusta; Tarraco; Barcino (main urban centres)
entries.hint: The Roman inscriptions of Hispania (over 20,000 surviving) include substantial numbers commemorating women — as wives, mothers, priestesses and occasionally in economic roles; the Hispanian inscriptions are among the richest evidence for Roman women's lives outside Rome and Italy.
entries.note: Hispanian inscriptions: EDCS (Epigraphik-Datenbank Clauss-Slaby) — digitised corpus of 20,000+ Hispanian inscriptions. The Emerita Augusta female priests (flamines): multiple inscriptions document women in religious office. Dixon (1992), The Roman Family: uses Hispanian inscriptions as evidence. The mosaics at Italica depicting women: primary visual evidence. Curchin (1991), Roman Spain ch.7 on social life.

events.l: Battle of Munda — Caesar's last battle
Flavian grant — Latin rights for all
Council of Elvira — first Western council
Priscillian — first Christian executed for heresy
